did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the YOLOv5 deep learning model (based on CNN architecture) to classify skin cancer types (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and seborrheic keratosis) using hyperspectral imaging data from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a YOLOv5 deep learning model for classifying skin cancer using hyperspectral imaging data from the ISIC dataset. It includes specific sections for Methods (data preprocessing, model training), Results (confusion matrices, performance metrics like precision, recall, accuracy), and Conclusions based on new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of Skin Cancer Using Novel Hyperspectral Imaging Engineering via YOLOv5
brief_summary: This study developed a rapid skin cancer detection method using the YOLOv5 deep learning model combined with hyperspectral imaging (HSI) technology to classify basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and seborrheic keratosis (SK). The dataset from the ISIC library was utilized, with images preprocessed into hyperspectral narrow-band images (HSI-NBI) and compared against standard RGB classification models. Model performance was evaluated using precision, recall, specificity, accuracy, and F1-score derived from confusion matrices. Experimental results indicated that the HSI model achieved a recall rate of 0.794 for SCC, representing a 7.5% overall increase compared to the RGB model's recall of 0.722. The study concluded that HSI-based conversion algorithms effectively highlight lesion features and improve detection capabilities for skin cancers, particularly SCC, compared to conventional RGB images.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary skin cancer types studied and classified using YOLOv5 on ISIC dataset images. Included in training (654 images) and validation (168 images) sets.

One of the three primary skin cancer types studied and classified. The HSI model showed improved recall (0.794) for SCC compared to RGB (0.722). Included in training (336 images) and validation (90 images) sets.
One of the three primary skin conditions studied and classified. Included in training (480 images) and validation (126 images) sets.
Used as a negative class in the confusion matrix evaluation for both RGB and HSI models to distinguish lesions from normal skin/background.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Seborrheic keratosis) and Background.
all_task_types.details: The study uses YOLOv5 to classify skin cancer types (BCC, SCC, SK) and background from hyperspectral narrow-band images (HSI-NBI) and RGB images. Performance was evaluated using confusion matrices, precision, recall, specificity, accuracy, and F1-score for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) library used to detect and classify skin cancer. Images were preprocessed into hyperspectral narrow-band images (HSI-NBI) and standard RGB images for model training and testing.

use.training: Used to train the YOLOv5 model. 80% of the data (1470 images) was divided for training after image amplification.
use.validation: Used to determine loss function curves after 300 iterations of training.
use.testing: Used to evaluate model performance. 20% of the data (384 images) was used for testing, with results analyzed via confusion matrices and metrics like precision, recall, specificity, accuracy, and F1-score.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the detection performance of the YOLOv5 model by calculating true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each class (BCC, SCC, SK, Background).
To quantify model accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score for both RGB and HSI models to compare their classification capabilities.

To compare the performance of the Hyperspectral Narrow-band Image (HSI-NBI) model against the standard RGB classification model, specifically noting the increase in recall rate for Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (precision, recall, specificity, accuracy, F1-score) derived from confusion matrices, and comparisons between the RGB and HSI models were made based on these metric values without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset.experiment: Comparison of RGB vs HSI-NBI models using YOLOv5
ISIC Dataset.notes: The HSI model showed an overall increase of 7.5% in recall rate for SCC compared to the RGB model (0.722 to 0.794). The HSI model also improved specificity for the SCC category and specificity for the SK category compared to the RGB model. Accuracy rates were similar between models (0.792 for RGB vs 0.787 for HSI).

architecture_details.backbone: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) composed of Focus, CONV-BN-Leaky ReLU (CBL), Cross Stage Partial (CSP), and Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP) models. Specifically retains CSP1_X structure of YOLOv4 version CSPDarknet-53 and adds CSP2_X structure.
architecture_details.neck: Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) and Path Aggregation Network (PAN) consisting of CBL, Upsample, and CSP2_X layers.
architecture_details.head: Bounding box regression, confidence, and classification heads.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to a default size of 640 × 640 pixels to ensure consistent format and avoid memory issues
Image annotation using LabelImg software to generate XML files
Conversion of annotation files to TXT format for YOLOv5 input
Conversion of annotated images to Hyperspectral Narrow-band Images (HSI-NBI) using a spectrum conversion algorithm
Analysis of normalized reflection spectrum of the lesion area to compare reflected light intensity differences
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce dimensionality and decorrelate spectral information
Selection of specific wavelength bands (380–780 nm, 405–435 nm, and 525–555 nm) to extract important lesion features
Image amplification (data augmentation) for the training and test sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training and test sets after dividing the data into 80% for training and 20% for testing. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) were not reported in the text.

conclusions: The HSI-based conversion algorithm effectively highlights lesion features and improves detection capabilities for skin cancers, particularly squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), compared to conventional RGB images.
The HSI model demonstrated a superior ability to learn SCC features, resulting in a recall rate increase from 0.722 (RGB) to 0.794 (HSI), representing a 7.5% overall improvement.
The HSI model significantly improved the specificity for the SCC category and the specificity for the seborrheic keratosis (SK) category compared to the RGB model.
The overall accuracy rates of the RGB model (0.792) and the HSI model (0.787) were similar, likely due to the relatively small number of images used in the study.
The HSI model can extract detailed spectral bands, reduce image noise, and highlight lesion features, making it a viable method for classifying basal cell carcinoma (BCC), SCC, and SK.
Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) had the lowest recall rate in the HSI model because its symptoms are similar to seborrheic keratosis (SK), leading to potential confusion between the two classes.

YOLOv5 is simpler to use than previous versions because it automatically scales input images to the required size, and its relatively small model structure achieves similar accuracy at a faster speed.

The proposed HSI conversion technique has potential for implementation in capsule endoscopy and video dermatoscopes to reduce the cost of hyperspectral acquisition systems by allowing RGB images to be converted to HSI images.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small dataset (1,854 total images) from the ISIC library, which the authors acknowledge limits the generalizability and accuracy of the results.
No external validation was performed; the model was trained and tested solely on the ISIC dataset, raising concerns about dataset bias and overfitting.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) to support the claimed improvements in recall and specificity between the RGB and HSI models.
The HSI model showed lower overall accuracy (0.787) and recall (0.726) compared to the RGB model (0.792 and 0.758), despite the authors highlighting the improved recall for SCC specifically.
There is a notable confusion between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Seborrheic Keratosis (SK), with BCC having the lowest recall rate (0.624) in the HSI model due to similar symptoms.
The hyperspectral images were not captured via physical HSI sensors but were algorithmically converted from RGB images, which may introduce artifacts or loss of true spectral information.
Data leakage risks are present as the study uses a public dataset (ISIC) without specifying rigorous cross-validation or ensuring strict separation of patient data across splits.
The study lacks clinical relevance validation as it does not compare AI performance against dermatologist experts in a prospective or comparative clinical setting.
